nativeRange.habitat: Coral reef caves and crevices on lagoon and seaward reefs, typically observed from shallow water down to roughly 36 m

decorPreferences: live rock with abundant caves and crevices

diet.notes: Generally accepts vitamin-enriched frozen mysis, frozen brine shrimp, and finely chopped meaty foods such as krill, raw shrimp, squid, clam, and mussel. Has been documented foraging on amphipods and copepods from live rock, and a mature tank with a healthy pod population is commonly considered beneficial for long-term success. May benefit from targeted feeding while it acclimates.

imageCandidates.notes: Debra Baker; 1238x1172; sharp live in-situ individual clearly showing the reddish body, yellow bars behind head and at tail base, and blue-ringed eyespots (ocelli) diagnostic of Wetmorella nigropinnata.
Debra Baker; 1536x2048; live in-crevice habitat shot showing the species' cave-dwelling behavior; fish is small and partly obscured so kept only as a secondary context image.

summary: A tiny, secretive Indo-Pacific micro-wrasse with bold yellow bars and bright eyespots. Generally considered reef-safe and well suited to peaceful nano/pico reefs with plenty of rocky crevices to dart between.
careNotes:
The Yellow Banded Possum Wrasse (Wetmorella nigropinnata) is a small, cryptic labrid that tends to top out around 7–8 cm. It is generally considered reef-safe and one of the more aquarium-friendly wrasses for smaller systems. Standard reef water conditions suit it well: a temperature around 23–27°C, pH between 8.1 and 8.4, alkalinity (dKH) of 8–12, and a specific gravity of 1.020–1.025.

A minimum of around 100 L is generally advised despite the fish's tiny size. Aquascaping with abundant caves, overhangs, and shaded crevices is widely considered essential — without secure retreats specimens tend to remain hidden and can fail to feed.

This species has been reported to do well singly, as a pair, or as a small harem of one male and multiple females; males tend to display more intense coloration. Like most wrasses it is believed to be a protogynous (female-first) sequential hermaphrodite, meaning a dominant female can transition to male in the absence of one.

Wrasses are well known to jump, so a tightly fitting lid or mesh cover with no large openings is generally advised. Once acclimated, the fish gradually patrols the water column and the cave network.

Diet is carnivorous. Small frozen meaty foods — mysis, enriched brine shrimp, and finely chopped krill, shrimp, or clam — are generally well accepted. Naturally occurring amphipods and copepods on live rock have been documented as a regular food source, and a mature aquarium with an established pod population is commonly considered beneficial for long-term success. Newly arrived individuals may need targeted feeding for the first few weeks while they overcome their initial shyness.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of this species has not been reliably documented in available aquarium sources. Spawning in home aquaria is generally considered unlikely outside of dedicated breeder setups.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Product page reached via the wrasse-reef-safe collection landing page. Provided adult size (3 in), minimum tank (15 gal), temperature (72–78°F), pH (8.1–8.4), SG (1.020–1.025), dKH (8–12), care level (Easy), reef-safe (yes), diet, native origin (Indonesia), and notes on coloration and jumping behavior.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (3 in), minimum tank (24 gal), care level (Beginner), peaceful temperament, reef-safe (yes), diet (carnivore, large chunks of meaty foods including krill, raw shrimp, squid, clam, mussel), native range (Indo-Pacific and Red Sea), and confirmed jumping/lid warning. Also documented haremic behavior (1 male + multiple females) and male sexual dichromatism.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Labridae / Eupercaria misc / Cheilininae). Adult size 8 cm TL — slightly larger than retailers' 3 in (7.6 cm). Distribution: Indo-Pacific, Red Sea to Marquesan and Pitcairn islands, north to Ryukyu Islands, south to southern GBR and New Caledonia. Wild temperature 25.5–29.3°C. Habitat: lagoon and seaward reefs, in caves and crevices. Reports distinct pairing during breeding.

fish.breedingNotes: No reliable captive-breeding accounts found in available hobby or scientific sources.
